# Extractor Receipt

## Summary

This source contains empirical evidence about failure rates, failure taxonomy construction, annotator agreement, LLM-judge evaluation, and intervention results for multi-agent LLM systems across multiple frameworks and tasks.

## Evidence Files

### file: evidence-01.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-012
evidence_id: evidence-01
kind: result
citation: Cemri et al., Why Do Multi-Agent LLM Systems Fail? (Abstract)
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2503.13657
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: supports
---
Performance gains of multi-agent LLM systems on popular benchmarks were reported as minimal relative to single-agent frameworks.

## Source Excerpt

"Despite growing enthusiasm for Multi-Agent Systems (MAS), where multiple LLM agents collaborate to accomplish tasks, their performance gains across popular benchmarks remain minimal compared to single-agent frameworks."

## Note

This is a comparative high-level result stated in the abstract; the captured source does not provide benchmark-by-benchmark values for this claim in the quoted passage.
```

### file: evidence-02.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-012
evidence_id: evidence-02
kind: method
citation: Cemri et al., Why Do Multi-Agent LLM Systems Fail? (Abstract; Introduction)
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2503.13657
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: supports
---
The study analyzed five popular multi-agent frameworks across more than 150 tasks or traces using six expert human annotators.

## Source Excerpt

"We analyze five popular MAS frameworks across over 150 tasks, involving six expert human annotators."

"We analyze five popular open-source MASs, employing six expert annotators to identify fine-grained issues across 150 conversation traces, each averaging over 15,000 lines of text."

## Note

The source uses both "over 150 tasks" and "150 conversation traces"; both indicate study scale, but the exact unit varies across passages.
```

### file: evidence-03.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-012
evidence_id: evidence-03
kind: result
citation: Cemri et al., Why Do Multi-Agent LLM Systems Fail? (Abstract; Introduction)
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2503.13657
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: supports
---
The authors identified 14 distinct multi-agent failure modes and grouped them into 3 categories.

## Source Excerpt

"We identify 14 unique failure modes and propose a comprehensive taxonomy applicable to various MAS frameworks."

"These fine-grained failure modes are organized into 3 categories: (i) specification and system design failures, (ii) inter-agent misalignment, and (iii) task verification and termination."

## Note

This is the paper's central taxonomy claim and is repeatedly stated across the abstract and findings sections.
```

### file: evidence-04.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-012
evidence_id: evidence-04
kind: metric
citation: Cemri et al., Why Do Multi-Agent LLM Systems Fail? (Abstract; Introduction)
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2503.13657
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: supports
---
The taxonomy refinement process reached Cohen's kappa 0.88 in expert inter-annotator agreement.

## Source Excerpt

"This taxonomy emerges iteratively from agreements among three expert annotators per study, achieving a Cohen’s Kappa score of 0.88."

"To ensure consistency in failure modes and definitions, three expert annotators independently label 15 traces, achieving an inter-annotator agreement with a Cohen’s Kappa score of 0.88."

## Note

The agreement value is attached to the taxonomy validation process, not to end-task performance of the MAS systems.
```

### file: evidence-05.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-012
evidence_id: evidence-05
kind: metric
citation: Cemri et al., Why Do Multi-Agent LLM Systems Fail? (Introduction)
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2503.13657
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: supports
---
The paper reports that correctness for the open-source MAS ChatDev can be as low as 25%.

## Source Excerpt

"Our empirical analysis reveals that the correctness of the state-of-the-art (SOTA) open-source MAS, ChatDev (Qian et al., 2023), can be as low as 25%, as shown in Fig. 1."

## Note

This is a system-specific reported low-end correctness figure for ChatDev, not a universal rate for all MAS frameworks.
```

### file: evidence-06.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-012
evidence_id: evidence-06
kind: metric
citation: Cemri et al., Why Do Multi-Agent LLM Systems Fail? (Interannotator study)
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2503.13657
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
Initial inter-annotator agreement on the first sampled round was weak, with Cohen's kappa 0.24 before taxonomy refinement.

## Source Excerpt

"In Round 1, we sample 5 different MAS traces from over 150 traces we obtained with theoretical sampling as explained in the previous section, and the three annotators annotate these traces using the failure modes and definitions in the initial taxonomy. We observe that the agreement reached at Round 1 is very weak between annotators, with a Cohen’s Kappa score of 0.24."

## Note

This shows the initial taxonomy was not stable enough before iterative refinement, which bounds confidence in the earliest rubric version.
```

### file: evidence-07.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-012
evidence_id: evidence-07
kind: metric
citation: Cemri et al., Why Do Multi-Agent LLM Systems Fail? (Interannotator study)
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2503.13657
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: supports
---
After refinement, Round 2 inter-annotator agreement reached average Cohen's kappa 0.92 and Round 3 reached 0.84.

## Source Excerpt

"In the next stage (Round 2), we sample another set of 5 traces, each from a different MAS. Then, the annotators agred substantially well on the first try, attaining an average Cohen’s Kappa score of 0.92 among each other."

"Motivated by this, we proceed to Round 3, where we sampled another set of 5 traces and again annotated using the same finalized taxonomy, where achieved an average Cohen’s Kappa score of 0.84."

## Note

These values support improved rubric stability after revision across held-out trace sets.
```

### file: evidence-08.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-012
evidence_id: evidence-08
kind: metric
citation: Cemri et al., Why Do Multi-Agent LLM Systems Fail? (LLM Annotator; Table 2)
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2503.13657
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: supports
---
The few-shot o1 LLM-as-a-judge pipeline achieved 0.94 accuracy, 0.77 recall, 0.833 precision, 0.80 F1, and 0.77 Cohen's kappa.

## Source Excerpt

"Table 2: Performance of LLM-as-a-judge pipeline

o1 (few shot) 0.94 0.77 0.833 0.80 0.77"

"As we achieve an accuracy of 94% and a Cohen’s Kappa value of 77%, we deem that the LLM annotator, with in context examples provided, to be a reliable annotator."

## Note

The reliability claim is tied specifically to the few-shot configuration of o1 and the reported evaluation setup.
```

### file: evidence-09.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-012
evidence_id: evidence-09
kind: result
citation: Cemri et al., Why Do Multi-Agent LLM Systems Fail? (Introduction)
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2503.13657
confidence: medium
mechanism_relevance: direct
contradiction_relevance: supports
---
The paper states that many MAS failures arise from inter-agent interaction challenges rather than only limitations of individual agents.

## Source Excerpt

"Consistent with these theories, our findings indicate that many MAS failures arise from the challenges in inter-agent interactions rather than the limitations of individual agents."

## Note

This is an interpretive finding reported by the authors, but it is still directly stated as a conclusion from their analysis.
```

### file: evidence-10.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-012
evidence_id: evidence-10
kind: result
citation: Cemri et al., Why Do Multi-Agent LLM Systems Fail? (Introduction; contributions)
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2503.13657
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
Best-effort prompt and orchestration interventions improved ChatDev by 14 percentage points but did not eliminate failures and still left performance too low for deployment.

## Source Excerpt

"Our case study with AG2 (Wu et al., 2024a) and ChatDev (Qian et al., 2023) reveals that although these interventions yield a +14% improvement for ChatDev, they do not resolve all failure cases. Moreover, the improved performance remains insufficiently low for real-world deployment."

"Despite achieving an improvement of 14% on task completion, they fail to fully address MAS failures, highlighting the need for structural MAS redesigns."

## Note

This evidence contains both an improvement and an insufficiency claim; the source explicitly preserves the limitation, so downstream use should not present the intervention as a complete fix.
```

### file: evidence-11.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-012
evidence_id: evidence-11
kind: metric
citation: Cemri et al., Why Do Multi-Agent LLM Systems Fail? (Case studies; Table 4)
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2503.13657
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
In ChatDev case studies, ProgramDev accuracy increased from 25.0 baseline to 34.4 with improved prompts and 40.6 with new topology; HumanEval increased from 89.6 to 90.3 and 91.5.

## Source Excerpt

"Table 4: Case Studies Accuracy Comparison."

"Baseline ... ProgramDev 25.0 HumanEval 89.6"

"Improved prompt ... ProgramDev 34.4 HumanEval 90.3"

"New topology ... ProgramDev 40.6 HumanEval 91.5"

## Note

The gains are benchmark-specific and modest on HumanEval; the surrounding text says the interventions did not constitute substantial improvements.
```

### file: evidence-12.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-012
evidence_id: evidence-12
kind: claim-support
citation: Cemri et al., Why Do Multi-Agent LLM Systems Fail? (Conclusion; methodology)
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2503.13657
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
The authors explicitly state MASFT is a first step and do not claim it covers every possible multi-agent failure pattern.

## Source Excerpt

"We do not claim MASFT covers every potential failure pattern; rather, it serves as the first step towards taxonomizing and understanding MAS failures."

## Note

This caveat limits overgeneralization of the taxonomy and should remain attached to any downstream use of MASFT as a coverage framework.
---

We observe that the agreement reached at Round 1 is very weak between annotators, with a Cohen’s Kappa score of 0.24."

## Note

This shows the initial taxonomy was not stable enough before iterative refinement, which bounds confidence in the earliest rubric version.
```

Best-effort prompt and orchestration interventions improved ChatDev by 14 percentage points but did not eliminate failures and still left performance too low for deployment.

## Note

```
